name: An entirely expected surprise
content: Skin and bone and tooth and nail—frail as the powers that be—sag around bloody muscles. The carriage of my self trudges, then troddles, then walks up right. There are butterflies in my stomach scoring cacophonies to the dreams I should be having but can not afford. It is past the darkest day already. The hair on my arms and neck stand at ends and at odds with a sinking chest. I would like to throw up but I haven’t eaten so there is no body to expel. Bodily expulsions come as syllables and sound every so often from elsewhere around me; people are far and few in between, so I look up at a hazy-daze greyish sky and imagine a black bird. Accordion spines compress again but breathing is still an order tall. I walk, and the butterflies move along and afar with me. Indeed I should be wearing more, but I willed it to be sunnier than it is to night. Still, with a sigh and a shiver, I trudge and troddle and walk.

We have it moonless as you’d imagine. There are streetlights blasting streams of whites and blues into the eyes of my beholding. A distant firetruck wail grows fainter and darker and groans into inexistence. Parts and screws shudder and a bus engine moves along. I cough up empty steams of air with eyelids pinched together to avoid the enblackment. The warm clouds disperse and I remember that the evening is a chilly blister. Wind comes again and really does not seem to want to go at all. Right now I wish I was in someone’s arms. I want to be held. Van Gogh seems to have stolen my starry nights and the sky is empty. There are no fish in the rivers—not that I see, at least. I can swim but finding Nemo is not worth the effort.

The tote bag is quite heavy. I nod it along. There is inside it a laptop, a notebook, and a pen or maybe two. They discuss how this is entirely and squarely the fault of Benjamin Netanyahu. He emerges.
